column1: One of the first impressions that you get

column1: The Bab-Amman stands on the Roman road going south to Amman. To the left is a filled-in space where once there was a Naumachia for sham sea-fights and water pageants and beyond it was the Circus. The South Gate set in the wall of the city walls it appears to have been rather like an outer gate
column2: but very little of it remains. Inside the walls
column3: a little to the left of the gate
column4: the ruins of a peripteral temple stand on an artificial terrace on a sudden hillock. The southern wall of the Cella and some columns of the Corinthian order are still to be seen and are called by the Arabs the Beit et-Tei. Near to this temple is the Southern Theater; on the right is the graceful Ellipse of the Forum
column5: columns of Ionic order with the entablature so much in place that you scarcely regret that the oval is not complete.

column1: The great street of columns begins to the north-east of the forum and continues for about half a mile. Two roads intersect this main street which also are colonnaded and which have each a Tetrapylon at the crossing. Between these
column2: nearer to the further street
column3: stood the Great Propylaea of the Temple of Artemis
column4: which stood up on a terrace on rising ground of columns of the Corinthian order that once adorned the Portico are a striking feature of the general view. Near to the temple were various churches
column5: built of the old material. One of these is a fine basilica of the Byzantine period.

column1: The further colonnaded street was the street of the Theater. To the left was the Northern Theater
column2: which seems to have been for gladiatorial shows and other performances dear to the heart of the Roman soldiers. To the right the road ran down to the river where there was once a bridge. On this road were some magnificent Baths. The road which led to the temple was quite a short one; it was continued down to the river passing the site of a later Byzantine church. The first cross road
column3: which is the longest
column4: ran through the Eastern Gates to a steep

column1: flight of steps that led to another bridge over the river. Outside the gates
column2: north and south were sarcophagi; one large mausoleum
column3: that of Es-Samuri has a fine portal and some columns of the Corinthian order. Outside the Northern Gate is the ruin of a basilica that was built with the materials of the pagan sanctuary of Nemesis. This is the skeleton of the town.

column1: There are some remains of an inner line of columns that suggests
column2: that typical of Greco-Roman towns
column3: was surmounted by an open arcade on a level with the first story of the houses. The writer Libanus tells that in Jerash you need never stay indoors because of bad weather as you always had the shelter of the arcades.

column1: From the general layout of the town there was space enough for a Roman legion to have been accommodated. The Crusaders never tried to keep the place as no remains of their fortresses are to be found.

The Triple Gate (Bab-Amman) apparently stood without a connection with the outer wall. It just was a triumphal gate through which people had to pass on their way to Gerasa. The suggested date is that of the arch of Trojan in Rome to which it bears some likeness. It consists of a central arch with lesser openings on either side.
In general the capitals of the columns were mostly Corinthian and the acanthus design of their capitals were of the Roman development of the Greek acanthus
column2: but probably executed by Greek artists
column3: as was very usual in Siria. Whatever was done in this Greco-Roman city was done with the view to the vista and component of modern towns would have offended the Greek sense of the aesthetic order of space and proportions.

column1: Jerash has been said to be second only to Palmyra in size and importance and second only to Baalbeck in beauty of Roman architecture.

PETRA is a complex of roman ruins consisting of monuments and public buildings carved directly into the pink rock formation of a high and deep Wadi. There are periodic floods that through milleniums have formed this very deep irregular wide wadi. These floods are a hazard to the travelers in some places. Twenty-nine American tourist died because of it two years ago. The floods are also eroding the monuments. As a first step a tunnel for deviation of the waters has recently been built
column2: but this is only part of a protection project. This area is called the Basin. Beduins families
column3: 96 in number are living in the Basin and together with unorganized tourism are causing damage to the monuments. The opening of the wadi into the desert is called the Entrance. This area has many interesting egyptian tomb constructions.

column2: public buildings and monuments. It is located 45 minutes drive from Amman.

column1: Here in New York the World’s Fair is progressing. I don’t see how they can possibly have everything ready on time but maybe they will. My father has made the exterior mural and interior decoration for the theatre out there. Other than that there has not been much to do for it. It will be an interesting affair. Built on the “democratic” idea of free expression there are a few good things and some perfectly terrible ones. Quite a contrast to similar developments in Germany or Italy where one standard exists throughout. The proportions of the thing are staggering and it should be a very interesting thing to visit. They are planning on a two year run and I have heard rumours of even three years. The “theme” of the whole thing is rather a joke. Everybody feels it looks like Göring’s or failed reproductive organ and I must say I rather agree. It is certainly no architectural motive in my mind. More like a phallic symbol of older times. I believe it is be rather absurd that they did not exercise more control over the planning of the whole thing. When do you plan coming up to see it? If you have any friends who want to come warn them not to come during July or August as the heat out here it is is simply terrible during those two months.

Right now the U.S. is being thoroughly scared by the President and the newspapers regarding the dictators of Europe and the possibility of war. They could mind their own business over here and will have us as a war yet. I only hope that Roosevelt gets human and during the next election before he has us revengeing the Jews. The only reason anybody with the least bit of brains can see in his attacks on foreign dictators is to try to hide the misgivings of the present administration which so far has not been able to return to U.S. prosperity. With a lot of war industry they think they can put people to work. The people here are actually beginning to believe that Hitler wants to send bombers over here to attack and enter the U.S. The Germans are getting absolutely superhuman. And as far as South America is concerned the belief is prevalent that the Nazis are fast on the road to controlling business and trade and will soon be making colonies and you Chileans
column2: Bolivians
column3: Peruvians
column4: etc. When the Socialists lose and in Chile it will be just another proof of fascist conquests.

column2: you dog you
column3: say “hello” to everybody for me with particular greetings to your mother and father.

column1: Wish you all the success with your work.

column1: For three and a half months I have been living alone. My Mother has left for Europe for a year’s stay — mostly in Paris. A fireplace makes it even more attractive. I usually eat at home and find it very easy to broil steak
column4: etc.

column1: What is all this I have read in the papers about a baby revolution in Santiago? I had to go to Peru for an earthquake and come back to the States to hear about a revolution. Really you didn't have things planned very well for me.

column2: it has certainly been interesting to follow events in europe. All our old friends who would have loved to say to us """"I told you so"""". I wonder how they are all getting along these days. I am sure that some of them have met with some disappointments. For me the most encouraging thing of all was to hear that there would be no war. A war would have no doubt meant the end of the present system of civilization in europe. Here in New York the refugees keep pouring in faster than ever
column3: I only fear that a race prejudice drive may start here in the not too distant future. From Better and Schaller I have already received pleas to send Afidavits to make it possible for them to come over to this country. Not being assured of steady employment I can do nothing for them. There is one person who I often wonder about — Franz von Hildebrand. I hope that he was able to get out before they got him as he was on their black list.

column1: From Nora I hear quite often. She wrote that you had written to her some time ago and was very glad to think that you still thought of the family. Lately I have had a rather depressing letter from her. All their friends seem to have left Vienna with the result that she and her sister are rather left to themselves. No doubt the recent war scare had a lot to do with her state of mind.

Here in New York conditions seem to be improving a little although there is still comparatively little building going on/ My Father has been kept pretty busy with the result that I have enough to do. He recently completed a new Restaurant in the Empire State Building which is really very striking. I am sure that you would like it very much. The World’s Fair is progressing fast. Many of the buildings are up already. Practically all the buildings have huge murals on the outside. I don't believe that there has ever been a fair where the art work has been so """"en masse"""". Dad is doing the murals on the outside of the Theatre and Concert Building. He is using two metals (aluminum and brass) and mosaic. One of the figures is 12 meters high and a mask representing acting is of about the same height. He has also made the color scheme for the outside of the building and for the lobbies and theatre auditorium.
I still have my state examination to look forward to. It is still not determined if I will be able to take it this spring. When it is over I will feel new born.

One of the photos I took down on my visit to Lucho Valdez was accepted for publication in """"U. S. Camera 1939"""". It was one taken at the Rodeo San Fernandez of milling steers. The only reason I can feel proud of the fact of their accepting it is that they chose only two hundred pictures from over ten thousand which were submitted from all parts of the United States. During the summer I don't think I took over one roll of film which has at least saved me some money.

Please say """"hello"""" to everybody for me — especially to your Mother and Father who were so kind to me. I hope to still print the photos I promised L. Valdez and Tito. I will print the Berlenga pictures soon and send them to you by regular mail.
Let me hear from you again and not next year!";;;;Luis Mitrovic Balbontín;Winold Tjark Reiss;13-10-1938;NO;English;Manuscrito

I hope you are still alive to be able to read my letter of thanks for your Christmas letter of December 1997. I did mean to write to you last year (1998) but didn’t because last year was a very hard and very sad year for me. Étienne had started with small attacks of senile dementia some 3 years ago and this sad state became very violent at the beginning of 98. It was very sad to see my Étienne in this state and it was very difficult and strenuous to look after him. He was absolutely unrecognizable and I had to take leave of him bit by bit
column2: week by week
column3: month by month. There is no medicine or therapy to stop this degenerative illness. I am 83 years old and our doctor didn’t think I would be able to manage. Étienne refused any help from strangers and as he was up and about
column4: he fell frequently but couldn’t get up by himself and I couldn’t go on getting the Red Cross Ambulance to help me. So in August last year I managed to get him to a very good nursing home. He was not happy there because everything was strange to him. However he was not happy at home because even in our flat in familiar surroundings he was completely forlorn. He usually recognized me but not always. He was well looked after in the house and when he wandered and fell there were male nurses to pick him up. But he hurt himself frequently
column5: his wounds were well treated but he would
column6: in his complete ignorance
column7: tear off bandages. I visited him every day. In September one wound after a fall got septic. He was taken to hospital and I went with him. I knew when I saw him that this septicaemia was his end. I visited him 4 days at the hospital but he no longer moved
column8: his lovely blue eyes were open — without seeing — and I spoke to him and tried to hold his hand
column9: but there was no reaction and I knew he was going. I wanted him to die while I was with him. I was informed that he expired 20 minutes after I had left the hospital. Étienne was 92 years old. I can’t tell you how sorry I was for him in this dreadful state. His daughter and son came for the funeral and Étienne was cremated. Now I am alone in our flat but I will have to look for a place in a home for old people. I don’t mind being alone
column10: I never feel alone by myself and as I am still good at walking and love nature I go for walks in the immediate surroundings. In the past years I have learned a lot about nature: plants
column11: trees
column12: insects and

column1: animals but my favorite occupation has been for years — birdwatching. Last year I had little time for myself
column2: which I didn’t even notice I was so engaged looking after Étienne. And since his death there is an awful lot to do after a death
column3: which is just as well
column4: as one has less time for sorrowing.

column5: nothing can replace your companion Angela. Étienne and I were married for 48 years and I like to think of my Étienne in his good years. Thinking of last year makes me cry because I was so terribly sorry for him in this awful mental state.

Received your air mail letter upon my arrival in Lima. I thought that I had forgotten my ski-boots. Please send them to my address in the states 39 WEST 67 ST. right away as I shall surely want to go skiing a couple of times upon my return. But please send them registered - if possible morning one of the ""Santa"" ships so that they get to me quickly. In your next letter let me know the expenses and I will send the cash to you. My bags arrived in Lima some time ago and I thank you for seeing about them. I shall only see them on the ship as I don't want all the trouble of taking them through the Peruvian customs where they make me unpack everything to be sure one is not exporting silver a vicuña blankets. This next Wednesday
column3: the 29th
column4: I am sailing for Panama aboard the Santa Lucia. Received $300 - from home to get home on but that even does not cover the ridiculously high price of 1st class $345 - to N.Y. so I am going 3rd and using much of the money buying the marvellous ponchos one can buy here in Peru. If the 3rd class is too bad I shall change to a ship of the Dollar Line in Panama which has more reasonable prices. In the four days I spent in Lima I looked around for cargo boats but not many stop in Callao and those that do are few and far between and it doesn't pay for me to wait around as my father has much work and a job awaiting my return. Going through with the S. Lucia I shall be in New York
column5: January 10 if I change in Pan. a few days later. My trip in Peru has been very fine and certainly a fine finish to my three years away from the States. In my last letter I wrote you that I was taking the plane from Cuzco to Lima.

column1: Our takeoff was delayed a day by bad weather. The trip was very interesting
and in a very fine plane - a new Curtis Condor with capacity for 20 passengers
and piloted by American pilots. Much cheaper and quicker than by going
Thursday I left Lima for La Merced in a new Ford - sort of taxi arrangement
with 3 other passengers and very cheap (15.-soles) and very comfortable. La Merced
lies up in the district of CHANCHAMAYO - the tropics of Peru. To get there one must
first climb to 4800 meters and then descend on a road to La Merced which is
so narrow that there is one way traffic each day in alternate directions. And what
a road - I swear I have never seen anything like it - cut for miles into the
solid rock of steep cliffs where one slip of the driver means certain death.
Arch. Baumgarten and his talking of the mountain roads of Austria should take
a look at it. Although it is the rainy season I had luck with the weather and
I believe with the malaria. The one night I spent there was blessed by an earth-
quake (Erdbeben) which woke me and the rest of the town up. So at last I've
experienced that - I was afraid I'd be in S.A. without being in one. From La
Merced I came on here to HUANCAYO by """"Camion"""" which was rougher riding -
but without the fleas - of Portugal. Through perfectly glorious country again
climbing high up over the mountains with a view of the snow capped Andes.
All the time passing through picturesque Indian villages with their charming
little churches. It broke my heart not to be in my own car and be able
to get out to take photographs. Spent the night in a little hotel on the way

column2: Pampas
column3: in fine weather which has now changed

column1: to the heavy rain of the rainy season. My Spanish has improved immensely (of
necessity) and I can now get along finding out and saying what I want although
it takes some time and the grammar is all wrong. If I had a few more months I
could really learn something. Give my love and regards to everybody. Sincerely
so don't forget to ask for them there. Tjark.
PD: The book """"Franciskus"""" should arrive by mail and the other books will be deposited at the Cullen Bank in your name";Huancayo

column6: Uruguay to get out and apply for a visa there. But I do not know if the boat stops there and the agents of the Yugoslav Lloyd here will surely also not know. And then that it would mean buying an additional visa to Uruguay here

column8: and sailing 1st class from Montevideo to Buenos Aires. #2 Suggestion was to sail without my visa in the hope that you could arrange for a visa being granted to me upon arrival. (2) this being done by your getting in touch with your Agent in the Argentine telling them that I am invited to visit you in Chile and merely need a transit visa to get across the mountains & get to another boat to sail around through the St. of Magellan. As a member of the ships crew he'll be permitted to get off the boat but unless a visa is granted I will have to get back on the same boat when it sails. In other words I can get mad and attempt getting a visa by myself but help through possible influence would be no doubt necessary. I don't even know if they will take me from Patras under these conditions. If they do I will certainly have to guarantee that if I don't get a visa in Argentina I will return on the same boat to Patras to save the Company from being heavily fined. I hope you see my difficulty and the chance I will be taking of having to make a 31 day trip back to Europe which I certainly would not like. My added handicap will not being able to speak Spanish with these bastardly officials. If you have some particularly good friend going to Buenos Aires or in B.A. at about the time my ship arrives you might give em my address so that if need be I would have somebody who could help me out. I will be sailing on the Marija Račić (MARIJA RAČIĆ) between the 2nd and 7th of August and the crossing takes about 35 days. Well

column1: I would have given up the whole thing and gone home to get around to more pleasant topics and to answer the remarks in your letter. Your cousin Fanny has been very nice to me

I believe that she was a little disappointed that Heinz and I showed not a great interest in a new yacht club that has been started in Japan; but we really had little time to do so. The other cousin was the first to greet us upon our arrival and has said little since. The cousin who was in Vienna I have only seen like a ghost wandering about the halls still shuffling and not a word says. He hasn't recognized me so far but as soon as I get back I will immediately introduce myself again and see that he comes on at the same boat with us and drop him off to the Argentine authorities to worry about.

column1: I owe you so many apologies that I do not know where to begin. The letter with the photographs arrived and I can't tell you how much Mary and I both enjoyed them. The enjoyment didn't stop there as I showed them to many friends who were very much impressed with your beautiful country as well as with your ability as a photographer. Your Christmas greetings also came and I promised myself that I would sit right down and answer. Now your card has come and I am actually sitting down.

There have been some legitimate reasons for delay although not enough to thoroughly excuse me by any means. For some reason this has been a particularly bad winter for us as we have all been rather sick at one time or another. Our boy was very sick with pneumonia and that of course had us going for some time. When it hasn't been sickness I have been working nights and weekends on my own work.

When I was relieved from active duty in the Army in 1946 I thought that I had better get some practical experience in architecture so I took a job with an architect. Since then I have been with several different architectural offices and have also gradually been doing some of my own work on the side. I haven't particularly enjoyed working in offices but the experience has been most worth while and I have worked on things I would otherwise not have had the opportunity to work on.

About the only thing that I have done on my own which I have really had a lot of fun with was a ski development (small) built by a philanthropist down near Pittsburgh. The outlook is good there and I hope to get quite a bit more work from the same people. At present they are doing a Children's Zoo for which I am the consultant. The kind of jobs we would have done at the Academy.

I am sure that you must have done some wonderful work in Chile during the past few years. Perhaps some day I'll make enough money so that Mary and I can come down and see it. I certainly envy you and your country with its magnificent scenery. AND SKIING.

We got away for a few days last winter and it was the first time I had been skiing in nine years. You can imagine what I looked like. This year the snow here in the east has been so poor that there have only been two or three weekends for people to go away. The west must have gotten all of the snow this year.

Unfortunately we have no travels to match yours. Right after the war we did take a trip out west to California via the southern route. We saw a lot of the US I had not seen before and were particularly impressed with New Mexico
column2: Utah
column3: Nevada
column4: and Northern California.

column1: The past few summers we have contented ourselves with staying here in the east. Mary usually goes up to an old farm we have in the country and I go up weekends. We live in a very small apartment here in New York and hope to build a house soon where we will be in a more enjoyable surrounding and have a little more room. As you probably know it is very difficult to find a satisfactory place to live these days.

In one of your letters you gave me Knandle's address and I have mislaid it. I would like to write to him sometime. If you can bring yourself to answer this letter sometime please include his address again.

I received a letter from Mrs de Lüttken several days ago. She also mentioned having seen your friends and was quite surprised at the coincidence. She is planning to go to Peru shortly to some small town near Cuzco. Remarkable energy she seems to have. If she stays there I imagine that she will be getting down to Chile to see you. All her family seem to be quite well although I believe Mary and her husband are not too happy in Sweden and hope to leave soon also. Nora has a job she likes as secretary to the Austrian Minister. You probably already know all this from letters you have received.

Mary and I both send our kindest regards. I hope that you make a trip to the US soon so that we may have the pleasure of meeting your wife. Next time you send some photos please include one which does not require a microscope to see Angela. We would love to see some more pictures whenever you have some to spare.
I hope you do not mind receiving this letter written on the typewriter. You must remember how awful my handwriting is - no one can read it so I thought I would spare you the annoyance of trying.
Don't believe Tjark's excuse for using the typewriter! We just bought it a few days ago
column2: and he could hardly wait for a chance to use it. I do hope you manage to come. Please
column3: soon - I am most anxious to meet you.

column1: Best wishes to you both and my apologies again for having been so long to answer

column1: A hurried note because my ship the ""MARIJA RAČIĆ"" sails in an hour. Such a hurry because the boat is sailing earlier than was expected and they were kind enough to wait until I returned from Greece. Perhaps you will receive also a letter explaining the same things that are in this letter which I sent from Greece to Trieste that you would hear from me one way or another. Well my lad
column2: I am sailing without a visa first because as you know
column3: I could not get one in Vienna and second because they wanted to charge me $36.- U.S. for me in Greece. This because I have not lived one year in Greece which is a prerequisite for the ordinary fee of $7.- U.S. In the Argentine I will be able to get off the boat as a member of the ship's crew but if I don't get a visa in Buenos Aires I shall have to return with the same boat. I hope that in some way you can put your finger in the pie to see that I do get a visa. By all means let your people know that I am invited as your guest to visit you - and if you know somebody that has something to do with the Argentine in Buenos Aires who might be of help please let them know. It seems that arriving on a freight boat makes things a little more difficult because they believe that one wishes to immigrate. Whatever you do write to me care of Yugoslav Lloyd so that I know what to expect upon my arrival. If I don't receive a letter there I will expect my ""Poste Restante"" at care of the American Consulate.

column1: You Bastard you don't know me without work as you know how far I will get with my knowledge of Spanish. I have a little call on my consulate general as to who I am and asking him to give me what help he can but I don't know how far his help will go.
I hope you had a nice trip home and didn't lose your or my baggage. I shall leave you as a guest if after 35 days on this freighter I find that I have to return to Europe.

column1: Well Lucho I am sitting rather unfortunately. If I write as a real pessimist would I should say that we perhaps will not be seeing each other in Chile. column2: etc. etc. Don't worry my boy
column3: I have once actually lost my temper
column4: a kind anybody's feelings. The situation is rather a complicated one and I am with hopes of hearing it resolved at the Consulate in Greece. By law and specific directive the U.S. Consulate is not allowed to fill out and stamp these visa cards which the Argentine Consulate insists on having. The only way to get around this difficulty would be for the Argentine Consulate to use their discretionary powers and apply an article (number 6) of their visa regulations of Oct 17
column5: 1936 which states: ""la obligación de presentar las tarjetas consulares no rige para oficiales diplomáticos y consulares extranjeros acreditados en el país o en tránsito a través de su territorio
column6: ni para aquellas personalidades
column7: quienes siendo miembros del gobierno de una nación amiga o quienes por razón de su personal respetabilidad u ocupación
column8: hacen inmerecida la adopción de estas medidas para el objeto deseado"". However even after a letter from my consul general and Blanco's calling up Urquiza no result could be obtained. I have a letter from my consul in Vienna to our consul in Greece and hope that there I can obtain a visa. I should certainly be very sorry if I had to give up my trip to Chile because of stupid regulations.

column1: How are things in Santiago getting on? I suppose the apartment job will be coming along soon now and then you will be quite busy. So far I have had a marvellous time. That you have never been up in Bolivia or here to Cuzco via the Lago Titicaca means that you have missed a lot. You should by all means make the trip in the near future. The plane trip to Antofagasta was very beautiful and naturally interesting for me to see such barren country. By train to La Paz was one of the most beautiful trips I have ever made. You can't possibly imagine the coloring of the mountains near the border of Chile and Bolivia. And then the great white borax lake lying cradled between the volcanoes at sundown. Woke up the next day to look out upon the plateau of Bolivia (3800 meters) with little mud Indian huts
column2: colorfully dressed Indians
column3: and clouds that were just made for photographing. Groups of llama and sheep watched over by an Indian woman spinning her wool. Then towards evening the view down on La Paz 400 m. lower than the plateau and against a background of glacier covered mountains. Sunday the Indian market with hundreds of Indians sitting in rows selling everything from cocaine to used combs. During the week in La Paz I took a trip down to the tropics in a truck loaded with Indians and over one of the most dangerous looking roads I have ever seen. Eight hours for 150 kilometers with the road first climbing to 4700 m before descending to 1000. The destination was a little village called CHULIMANI - a place untouched by modern life as the road has only been finished a couple of years. The people didn't even seem to know what a camera was for they didn't move when I photographed them. Then over the Titicaca a few days later and on to Cuzco through country much similar to the plateau of Bolivia. A wonderful place is this Cuzco with the old Inca walls supplemented by the Spanish Colonial. Have visited many of the ruins in the vicinity
column4: the location of one of which - MACHU PICCHU - looks so impregnable on top of a mountain in subtropical country that one doesn't see how the Spaniards ever got to it. The Indians here both men and women wear costumes which remind one very much of pictures of the inhabitants of Mongolia and Tibet. Very nice things to buy woven out of the home spun wool or little things made out of silver. Today I leave by air for Lima if the weather improves. I shall probably stay there a week looking around for a ship to get home on and making a trip back up into the country. Best wishes to everybody in Santiago and to your Mother and Father in particular. Many thanks to you all for the nice visit you made possible for me. Best of luck and a good Xmas

column1: The acquaintance is a young woman by the name of Miss Content Peckham
column2: who is really a friend of a friend of mine. She works for Time Magazine which is owned by the same concern that publishes Fortune and Life Magazines. Her trip through South America is sponsored by the publication and I therefore suppose she is interested in certain features of Chile which you might be able to make easier for her to acquaint herself with. She has good connections up here in the States and I am sure that any favor you might be able to bestow upon her would be repaid on any visit you might make to New York and the U.S.A. column5: and all military units are being significantly reinforced with the latest weapons.

column2: there has been a steady upward development in Switzerland. Industry has picked up and in some mechanical sectors there is already a shortage of skilled workers becoming noticeable. Here too

column1: The devaluation of the Swiss Franc came unexpectedly suddenly. But there was no other way to prevent the massive speculation that the devaluation of the French Franc would have brought to the detriment of Switzerland. Then the monetary basis between London-Paris-New York was also taken into account. Holland and Switzerland were the last countries on the gold standard. It was no longer possible to hold both countries to it. The Swiss Franc is now covered at 120%. (Reichsmark 1.2%!) Import businesses have suffered major losses. Prices in Switzerland have not risen. Without government approval
column2: no wholesale or retail price may be increased. Therefore it is now favorable for foreigners on holiday (hotels). It is good that I still have money in it. Buying with German Marks would be madness
column3: 1 MK = 1.75 Fr.! The devaluation has not hit us hard. One should now be able to retrieve securities in England (30% profit).

column2: see above!) Your message finds me in great sorrow
column3: for my dear wife Pia
column4: with whom I was happily and harmoniously married for 36 years
column5: passed away a few months ago after a relatively short but serious illness. She died despite chemotherapy from Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (cancer). I am now suddenly alone at 76
column6: my wife was only 66 years old.

column1: But your letter was for me a great joy
column2: an enormous surprise. I had tried several times to reach you by letter in Santiago — how shall I tell you — later — but the letters came back from Chile via the local post. Today I am writing only briefly

column4: very interesting letter. In my files I looked back and saw that your last letter
column5: written on board the MS Dresden
column6: is dated 15.7.37! I will write to you in detail about ""my"" 50 years
column7: also about John Dijkstra etc. You still write quite excellent German. Lucho
column8: you are a true language genius! As I said
column9: a long letter will follow — for today many heartfelt thanks and friendly
column10: dear greetings from

column1: on 30 June 1991 I sent you a letter with pictures from Solothurn (with English commentary)
column2: and I now believe that you probably did not receive this letter
column3: as it apparently got lost in transit. Therefore I am sending you a copy of that letter here.

column1: A few more remarks about the Weimar period and afterwards:
Many years ago Frau Avenarius called me because she was on holiday in Switzerland by the lake near Thun
column2: staying with acquaintances. My wife and I then met Frau Avenarius in Bern and entertained ourselves over cake and tea in a tea room. But I no longer know any details of the conversation. She had not changed much
column3: was still as slim and tall as before. I enjoyed the reunion.

column1: Less pleasure was brought to me by a completely unexpected visit from Pepi zu Dohna in Solothurn. She looked ill and unappealing
column2: and it seemed to me she came from the nightlife underground of Munich
column3: where she would have been consorting with soldiers of the American occupation forces. I had the same miserable impression of a friend that Pepi brought with her. In any case I did not take Pepi home with me. There was nothing left to see of the former freshness and good looks of Pepi from the Weimar days.

column1: I fondly remember Frau Gräfin as a cultured lady
column2: and also how we listened together in your room to Ravel and Debussy records (Bolero and L'après-midi d'un faune). Sometimes I wonder whether in the cellar of the university so-called ""degenerate
column3: Bolshevist"" paintings might not have been stored
column4: such as works by Paul Klee
column5: Wassily Kandinsky
column6: etc. One could probably have acquired these paintings cheaply at the time. But then again
column7: who thought of that back then... The Nazis would have made difficulties. Today the artists of the former Bauhaus are of enormous value. Frau Gräfin knew these artists personally.

column1: Then I think of our amusing but mishap-filled trip in the old vintage convertible with you
column2: John and Bubi Laub to Meiningen. Bubi and his entire family were likely thrown into a concentration camp by the Nazi bandits and died there miserably — dreadful.

column1: For today I will close and hope that the letter will reach you in Chile.

as a freshly qualified architect

column7: as far as I know
column8: banned in Germany speaks in my opinion very strongly in favor of the WERK. According to my enquiries
column9: the WERK would cost per year (12 issues) to Santiago including postage and packaging 35.- Swiss Francs. Very good value

column11: post

Now I wish you for your great sea voyage to ""wintry Chile"" all that is pleasant and good. Hopefully you will find all your family in the best of health upon your return. The joy of the great reunion will be enormous
column15: for your homesickness will have grown considerably since our Weimar days. Are you travelling directly on the DRESDEN through the Panama Canal to Valparaiso
column16: without a stop?

column2: do also not forget to report occasionally on your doings and activities in your home surroundings

column2: wishing you once more a good journey and a fortunate sea voyage

column11: I had to tell him that you are

column1: It is pleasing that your dear wife is doing well again after treatment by specialists in Boston. One must be glad that no serious illness was found. I can imagine that the difficulties with the eyes
column2: that is
column3: with the vision for your wife
column4: are not easy to bear. I know this quite well from my own experience. Though even with magnifying glasses I do not see any better.

column1: Let me tell you something about our family: My wife Pia came from the family of a Federal Councillor (Minister). We married in 1953 and had two children
column2: a son and a daughter. The son became a jurist (abogado) and works at the State Department for Education. His name is Dieter and he is not yet married. My daughter Sabin became a teacher (maestra) and also studied at a school of arts and crafts for graphic design and photography. She is married to a banker who works in Zurich at a major bank
column3: Credit Suisse. I already have 2 grandsons named Tim (3 years old) and Bastian (1 year old). This then is my family
column4: but sadly my dear wife Pia passed away in April 1989 at the age of only 66 from Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma. It is very hard for me to live alone
column5: after we had a very happy marriage and experienced so much that was beautiful together.

column1: I received your letter with thanks. On the 28th of February I departed for Düsseldorf. Miss Italia accompanied me by taxi to the station and at the end the following mishap occurred. Miss Italia came with me into the express train and the train departed just as she had not yet got off. She rode willy-nilly with it as far as Erfurt and entertained the entire compartment in the train with certain conversations to the amusement of all fellow travellers.

column2: after a terribly tedious room search
column3: I finally found a decent abode within 2 days. The atelier
column4: which admittedly is not very large here in Düsseldorf but has great commissions
column5: pleases me very much. Unfortunately there is no construction currently underway here
column6: that is
column7: nothing being built outside
column8: the building work begins only when I am away again. As the greatest project we have underway is a 6-storey steel skeleton office building for the Vereinigte Deutsche Stahlwerke (United German Steel Works). The building is being constructed in phases over several years. In 1936 only a small part of it will be built. Completely new on a large scale in this building is the ceiling heating system
column9: that is
column10: not the floor that is heated
column11: but the warmth radiates evenly downward from the ceiling. Furthermore we are drawing the plans for two exhibition houses for a housing exhibition in 1937 in Düsseldorf. Then a garage conversion etc. In Berlin the Haus Riefenstahl is being completed. Petersen is also a great sportsman and appeared in various winter sports films for the UFA.

column1: Things are now quite lively here
column2: since troops [illustrated with small drawings of soldiers and a tank] have come into the Rhineland. The ""special"" girls and the architects are naturally pleased about this
column3: since new barracks etc. are needed — insofar as they have not already been built under a false name.

column1: Regarding the matter of the schools
column2: I was able to speak with a colleague from the atelier who previously worked for many years at Fahrenkamp's.

column1: After 7 years of working together with Prof. F.
column2: he knows the conditions in the academy and private atelier quite well. He said that they have already had such cases where students were employed alongside the academy also in private ateliers. Since Fahrenkamp
column3: judging by the type with the abilities of the person in question
column4: probably often receives such enquiries
column5: he said it would be most purposeful to speak directly with F. yourself (possibly presenting drawings). Fahrenkamp currently also has large commissions and would naturally have taken on personal work heavily
column6: and written enquiries cannot always be handled very individually. A personal introduction is therefore more advantageous.

column1: If you feel like making a visit to Düsseldorf that would truly be worthwhile
column2: it would please me. The Rhineland is the German region where spring first makes itself felt
column3: already now the crocuses are in bloom. Architecturally there is much modern work to be seen. In ladies' fashion I believe that a breath from Paris makes itself noticeable here
column4: since the dresses and what is inside them flow already a certain quantum of ""chic"" and ""charm."" What may further interest you is the following. In the house where I live
column5: a young architect has simultaneously taken an apartment
column6: with whom I struck up an acquaintance. This gentleman studied at the Bauhaus Dessau and has his complete study works here. I was allowed to look through all the works and I was knocked out with astonishment! They have achieved very much more
column7: not only in ""painting pictures"" as one might think
column8: but above all in construction — reinforced concrete
column9: statics
column10: etc. I was delighted by the systematic approach that was embedded in the designs under Prof. Mies van der Rohe. In short
column11: I have seen what was achieved and can now reject the mere attacks on Weimar etc.
column12: for I have seen the truth with my own eyes! Where are you now? If you should happen to come here we could then travel back to Weimar together? I could before my departure insert a few free days here for travelling around the Rhine region. Now wishing you much pleasure and warm greetings

column1: I thank you for your interesting report from Viana do Castelo. The letter had already been lying at home for some time and could not be forwarded to me
column2: as after the official end of the student labor colonies I was still wandering around privately with colleagues in the Swiss south. Is it not very amusing that threads from Portugal through your friend should now spin their way precisely to Solothurn
column3: that small town. There are very many Lüthy here
column4: but this family name is very strongly represented in Switzerland. The lady in question
column5: D.L.
column6: lives as far as I know outside the town of Solothurn in the small village of Feldbrunnen. I believe the villa is called Villa Serdan and I remember it
column7: as in the garden at the entrance two large flamingos or storks made of formed white-painted sheet metal stand
column8: which made a great impression on me as a child. The deceased husband of this lady was
column9: I believe
column10: previously active in Sumatra.

column2: I was programmatically three weeks in the student labor colony. I am satisfied with it and my expectations were not disappointed. Spengemann came too
column3: but unfortunately had to travel home to Bielefeld (Obernstr. 15) after just 14 days. He had to report immediately to the military and must now serve a full two years with the air force in Königsberg. Naturally his studies are interrupted for this time. As you know
column4: the obligatory military service in Hitleria has recently been suddenly extended to 2 years.

column1: Now to the labor service. We built a road approximately 1.75 m wide for mules along the southern slope of a mountain valley at 1400 m altitude. The work was not particularly hard and the atmosphere at work was ideally good. We were about 40 men
column2: of whom 14 were foreigners — English
column3: Czechs
column4: Poles
column5: Germans
column6: French. Added to these were 4 female students who worked in the kitchen
column7: then a young colonial doctor
column8: a colony leader
column9: a head cook and an engineer from the state for the technical supervision. The food was excellent and considering the primitive kitchen facilities outstanding
column10: actually even better than in Weimar. Through the physical work and the mountain air
column11: the appetite was enormously stimulated like an aperitif
column12: and the quantities of food one stuffed down one's throat were gigantic. Student-friendly grocery stores gave us much smoking material
column13: canned goods
column14: pineapple etc.
column15: which was then usually eaten after the campfires that took place twice a week. At the campfires
column16: productions of all kinds were performed; the foreigners with their peculiar songs etc. found a particularly grateful audience. The weather was fabulous. We usually worked only in swimming trunks and were consequently tanned brown by the sun. In our free time we bathed in the waterfall of the quite cold glacier stream. The few inhabitants of these valleys speak almost exclusively Italian and French. This comes from the fact that most people move to Paris in winter
column17: where they occupy themselves with selling roasted chestnuts

column1: After the camp I had actually planned a smaller Tour de Suisse by car or bicycle. Due to Spengemann's earlier departure I had to change my plans and so I moved for a few days to Lago Maggiore
column2: to Locarno
column3: Ascona
column4: Ronco etc. We bathed
column5: rowed and lazed about and enjoyed the magnificent weather. The southern Switzerland
column6: mainly Ascona
column7: is heavily visited by Germans; many sinister types of Jewish emigrants populate the otherwise so idyllic Ticino villages. I did not miss visiting the Hotel Monte Verità in Ascona
column8: about which I have already told you. It has now been very attractively enlarged and provided with a locally appropriate Locanda ticinese. The hotel is extremely tastefully furnished and from the von der Heydt collection one finds various works by Picasso
column9: Matisse
column10: Hodler
column11: Feuerbach
column12: which have been used to furnish the various reception rooms. Oriental art is also well represented
column13: so one finds numerous Indian deity sculptures and Chinese tapestries. The entire hotel operation makes a very cultivated impression overall
column14: though I hardly believe that all guests know what they are seeing and appreciating. Overall
column15: through this Fahrenkamp hotel building a new hotel type was created. The stiff life of the grand hotels is frowned upon
column16: only their comfort has been retained. Dress code does not exist.

column1: That Portugal possesses so many fabulous architectural works of art I had hardly believed
column2: since one hears so very little about this country. Travelling in Portugal is according to your description very romantic; fleas and bedbugs were also not entirely absent in our camp either. We christened our hut with the fine name IL CIMICIAIO. That one can still notice Gothic influences in such a westerly exposed country surprises me. Are you managing well with the Spanish language
column3: even in the remote fishing villages? Are you and Winold Reiss the only ones travelling there
column4: have the others remained in France? What does one notice of the Spanish Civil War near you? At first one believed here it was a trifling little revolution as one is accustomed to from Spain
column5: but the matter has developed into a gruesome internal war in which the plebs bestially vents itself.

column1: Some considerable time has passed before I come back to your letter
column2: but since there was nothing significant to tell I simply waited. I thank you for the interesting account of the ""Balkan excursion"" of Dr. [small drawing of a lamp/light] and the other amusing and tragic experiences during the winter holidays. I have not yet bought my camera. Based on your Exakta description I had it demonstrated to me in various first-class photography shops
column3: so that after the Leica and Rolleiflex system it seemed to me to be an ideal camera too; however all the photo dealers urgently advised me against buying such a camera
column4: since up to today the Ihagee-Werk had not been able to establish itself with any apparatus
column5: and countless complaints had made the earlier Exaktas very unpopular. The entire apparatus with lenses was said to be very imperfect and technically far behind the Leica and Contax. I mentioned your combination with Zeiss lens
column6: whereupon they explained that even the Zeiss lens
column7: as a first-class product
column8: could never compensate for the technical imperfections of the rest of the apparatus. I now believe I will have to decide on a Leica f 1:2 50mm after all.

column1: Enclosed you will find 2 model photographs of the Kantonsschule in Solothurn. Last Sunday the construction was finally approved by popular vote and will now provisionally be built to 2/3 of it on the basis of the approved credit. The upper photograph shows the rear side of the school. The 3 laterally fully glazed sections contain the stairwells
column2: while the other 2 contain the toilet facilities. Far to the left [small drawing of a building] on the outside lies the assembly hall (singing and concerts) with the logical [small drawing of a trapezoid floor plan] floor plan and the rising floor. The assembly hall is placed freely on supports and the space underneath is enclosed with wire glass and contains the bicycles and motorcycles of the students. The low wing parallel to the avenue contains cloakrooms and showers for the adjoining gymnasium.

column1: The lower photograph shows the front facade facing south-east
column2: the most favorable orientation from a school-technical standpoint for theory classrooms. The windows are still somewhat very large and will be changed. In order to break up the monotonous front the building was divided into 4 sections by incisions
column3: whereby the corridor on the rear side is connected to the south front each time by balconies.

column1: The model 1:200 is made of cardboard and has been executed with the greatest precision down to the smallest details. Since I only have these photographs here
column2: I ask you to return them to me occasionally. In a few years I will then be able to send you pictures of the school in natura.

column1: Have you by the way heard that Gropius was appointed to the great university Harvard in Massachusetts
column2: U.S.A.? He will have much to tell there about New Germany! At Easter I will travel to Holland and see whether the tulips are already blooming in Haarlem; one cannot however make any great leaps without first going through the tedious foreign currency formalities. Curiously
column3: in February I unexpectedly received a letter from Amsterdam
column4: it was from van Jautens' fiancée! I had namely wanted to convince myself in November by postcard whether this fellow had actually undertaken such an Africa tour
column5: to bring yet more light into this mysterious affair etc. The fiancée wrote therefore that Henry has actually been working for 9 months in Johannesburg in South Africa at an architect's and gave me his exact address. He wants to come home this year in order to get married!

column1: Have you made many new acquisitions for your gramophone
column2: though in Vienna the opportunity for good concerts should not be lacking and therefore the gramophone can rest somewhat. In Weimar we once listened to the record ""Rugby"" by Arthur Honegger
column3: but His Masters Voice has brought out several more records of this modern composer such as ""Skating Rink
column4: ""Roi David
column5: etc. I have recently heard these records in Switzerland. Here in Wuppertal too one has the opportunity to attend good concerts; I recently heard an excellent performance by Gaspar Cassadó. Also a Chopin cycle by Raoul Kochalski could satisfy me very much.

column1: In the meantime I interrupted the writing and was in Elberfeld at a great variety evening with ""Grock"" and a first-class Berlin artists ensemble. Grock as a musical clown was magnificent
column2: we Swiss can be proud of our compatriot.

column1: As an enclosure I have copied 3 sketches for you from the Schweizerische Bauzeitung not with all its small details
column2: but rather just to show you how leading Swiss architects work. It concerns the first prize-winning project for a congress building for the city of Zurich on the Alpenquai on the lake. It was a competition among Zurich architects. The task was enormously large and the old Tonhalle halls had to be cleverly connected with the congress hall for 2000 persons
column3: restaurants with restaurant terraces
column4: banquet hall
column5: garden
column6: foyer and vestibules
column7: chamber music hall etc. etc.

column1: What I actually want to show you is more the manner and approach of the overall concept
column2: above all how completely differently one tackles a task of these dimensions here compared to for example in [Germany]. One finds no trace of forced symmetry
column3: one tries with loosely arranged
column4: staggered building volumes to avoid the impression of the oppressive and colossal. One willingly foregoes the outward effect of gigantic surfaces and openings and consciously chooses the more intensive effect of a small-scale human dimension. The sketch — a perspective cross-section through foyer and congress vestibule — shows a generous spatial arrangement using all contemporary building materials and is despite its great dimensions airy
column5: fresh
column6: free and has no brutal monumentality.

column1: In 1939 the Swiss National Exhibition will be in Zurich. The overall plan is already available and it appears that on the ideal grounds by the lake an exhibition technically and architecturally outstanding is emerging. The Swiss Pavilion in Paris on the Seine also came about through a competition and should turn out quite well
column2: as it is being executed by a very good architectural firm in Basel. Naturally there will be no gigantic plaster fake-facades and Greek capitals to be found there
column3: but rather functional
column4: elegant
column5: light steel-glass-wood constructions!

column3: but my inner voice says no!!!! For today I want to stop and receive

column1: I am of Chilean nationality. In Chile I obtained my Abitur. At the Academy of Fine Arts I studied architecture for two semesters.
I had the intention of continuing my studies here in Germany and also completing my final examination as an architect. Having heard of the first National Socialist Hochschule für Baukunst in Weimar
column2: I had a prospectus sent to me. According to this prospectus the school seemed very suitable for my further studies. In Weimar I was very disappointed
column3: because most of the points mentioned in the prospectus did not correspond to the facts.

column1: What mattered to me above all was to get to know the National Socialist architectural philosophy in the new Germany
column2: and I was unfortunately very disappointed in this regard in the lectures of Prof. Schultze-Naumburg.

column1: It is regrettable that this school
column2: which is beginning to seek recognition abroad through exaggerated statements in prospectuses and through many newspaper advertisements
column3: has disappointed me so. For this reason I have submitted my withdrawal.

I had no opportunity to say goodbye to you before my departure from Weimar. That I am working here on the Baltic Sea during the holiday period I have already told you. Unfortunately I am not allowed to write to you anything about the nature of the assignment area
column3: since the letter is going abroad. The work is very interesting. I have a 70-meter long building to work on completely independently. In this building a district heating system is planned
column4: from which a large number of other buildings will be heated. So very interesting and instructive.

column1: My working hours are fabulous. It starts at 7 in the morning until 3 in the afternoon. Saturdays until 12:30. Afterwards I jump into the Baltic Sea every day. That gives me a great deal of fun. All the Baltic Sea resorts are quickly reachable from here
column2: one can also get to Sweden very cheaply. Beautiful women there are in vast quantities. I go off with a different one almost every day. When one sees these sinful figures in bathing suits daily one cannot do otherwise. It suits me excellently though. My salary is also decent. I no longer need to budget myself as carefully as in Weimar. When you write to Rolf
column3: please give him my regards. I have not seen him either anymore
column4: his address has gotten away from me.

column2: I was tremendously pleased about your letter. I already imagined you among the fighting troops in Spain. Your mother is after all of Spanish nationality? Your summer journey was certainly more beautiful. You have again seen a great deal.
now you will be astonished my boy! You think I am in Stralsund or Weimar. But I have been since the 1st of September in the atelier of the most occupied Munich architect
column3: Prof. Roderich Fick
column4: working as an assistant. Morkus had written to me in Stralsund and asked me to join Fick and interrupt my studies. Fick is today one of the best architects in Germany and private architect of the Führer. We have only personal commissions of the Führer to work on. Very fabulous
column5: interesting work. You have often told me before about beautiful Austria. It was always my wish also once to get to the mountains. Now I have the luck to be allowed to work in the beautiful city of Munich. To Garmisch-Partenkirchen I travel quite often at the weekend. I have also already been to Innsbruck and Salzburg. Salzburg is a very
column6: very beautiful city. Enchanting. Where are you spending the end of the year again
column7: like last year?

column1: That Prof. Peter Behrens has been appointed to Berlin I have read; Popp will be a good replacement for him. Your competition work is very fine
column2: I would like to see the solution once. In June you will already have your diploma and will then be active as a great architect in your homeland.

column1: What probably interests Fick the most is what is happening now in Weimar. The same story as before. Morkus has requested his dismissal and maintains here in Munich a larger office. Prof. Fick has handed over to him some very fine commissions. Raiser will also soon be leaving Weimar
column2: as a better position has been offered to him in Stuttgart. Beblo maintains a craftsmen and carpentry school in Berlin. So everything is falling apart. The leadership of the university still lies in the hands of Prof. Schultze-Naumburg. As replacement for Morkauer
column3: Prof. Mühlfeld has been appointed as design teacher. A replacement for Raiser is not yet there
column4: and Beblo is being represented by Dipl. Arch. Hänsler. A new young architect
column5: a Schmittheimer student
column6: has also been appointed to the school. You see
column7: a dreadful continued governance by Messrs. Schultze and Koch. Whether I will ever return to Weimar I do not know.

column1: Sorry for not answering your letter of June 5 a bit earlier
column2: but I was waiting for a clearer picture as to my possibilities to make use of your kind invitation to visit you this year. I regret very much that I finally had to decide not to make such a major trip this year.

column1: Lately business has been very slack for me and I don't intend to continue much longer. However there are several deals still in ""the pipe-line"" and I don't want to endanger my commission on those deals. Under these circumstances I feel that I should not leave on a trip abroad lasting some weeks.

Not helping matters is the present bad situation at the stock-exchange. I invested a substantial amount of money in an airline and a meat-factory. Both of them went bust. On most of my other shares I also had heavy losses
column2: but
column3: of course
column4: being an optimist I believe that there soon may be a recovery.

column1: I don't remember if I gave you the full story on my skin cancer. It all started with a small ulcer at my left temple. The surgeon told me after removal that it was not malign. But very soon after that a second ulcer developed and this time it was ""a little"" malign. A third ulcer was then removed close to my left eye. This operation left me with extreme swollen eyelids
column2: for which there was no cure apparently and I finally had to have my eye removed. Then a tubercle was discovered under my (removed) eye and again I was prepared for an operation
column3: but after the surgeon had already given me a full anaesthetic he decided that he would not operate
column4: but hand me over to the radio-therapeutic section. I had then 40 radiations over a period of one month. It was a terrible experience
column5: causing great pain
column6: especially in my mouth. The radiation stopped mid April this year. I now got my prothesis and I also have new spectacles with slightly tinted glasses. I enclose a recent picture.

column2: I had a broken leg in Germany. Do you also remember the drive in my Dixie car
column3: when we had one break-down after the other? Rolf was then also in the party.

column1: A few days ago I went to the Floriade. It was really magnificent. That day the weather was also perfect. I walked for 6 hours and then I had probably seen not more 1/3 of the show.
Last month I went by train to Berlin and from there to the ILA at the Schönefeld Flugplatz. The ILA (Internationale Luftfahrt Ausstellung) was previously always held every two years at Hannover
column2: but since the German unification it has been removed to Schönefeld.

column1: I'm quite unhappy about the decision I had to take about not going to see you this year

column1: After my last telephonecall with Rolf
column2: I knew that he could not possible live much longer. Life was for him not worthwhile living anymore with all the ailments he had.

column1: I hope that I shall have the opportunity to visit Switzerland again this year and that I then shall meet with Rolf's son Dieter.In previous letters I told you already of medical operations I had to undergo. At the 4th operation
column2: the surgeon had to remove my Left eye. This was however not yet sufficient to stop my skin cancer and I had to undergo 40 radiations. I will only know after quite some time
column3: if these radiations will have had the required effect. At the moment I'm slowly recovering from the after effects of the radiations. I much preferred to undergo 4 operations to the radiations.On the 11th of this month they will start on making an artificial replacement for my left eye and surroundings. After completion
column4: it should be difficult to see
column5: that I'm handicapped.

column1: Just before my last operation I had my cardriver's licence renewed for 5 years. I now have to make a new application
column2: but I'm confident that I shall be allowed to continue driving my car.Your travel descriptions are not at all boring to me. On the contrary they are very interesting and I followed them on my map. I'm very interested to see at least a bit of your country during a few weeks. To really see your country might take more than 6 months.

column1: At the moment I can not decide if I can make use of your very kind invitation to come and stay with you. I expect to let you know by about August next
column2: if I can come in either December or January. I'm particularly keen on meeting you again after such a long time. We will no doubt have lots to talk about.It must have been hard on you to have had twice pneumonia in the course of one year. I also had twice pneumonia when I was a child. At that time it was still a very risky disease.We have now lovely spring weather. The famous ""Keukenhof"" flower garden is open and attracting people from all over the world as also does the ""Floriade""
column3: a huge horticulture and flower display held only once in 10 years.Sofar this year I have not been able to travel
column4: but I expect that I shall start making short trips abroad in June. I don't feel the missing of my left eye as a great handicap
column5: but all the same shall I make less use of my car and use the train instead.The enclosed picture shows you me
column6: next to a picture of my father on the wall (born: 1965). The picture was taken last month and shows clearly no beardgrowth on the left side of my face
column7: where I was radiated. I'm now anxiously waiting for some sign of growth.Kindest regards

column1: From Rolf Altenburger I recently got your address. What a surprise
column2: to hear from you after more than 50 years.

column1: How are you? What have you been doing all these years? Now that it has become a bit more pleasant to visit East-Germany
column2: I may go soon to Weimar and have a look at the ""Haus in der Sonne"".

column1: Since I left Germany in 1937 I have done a lot of flying. My logbook shows about 16.000 flying hours. During the last world-war I was flying in the British Royal Air Force and at the end of the war I joined KLM and flew for them during 10 years
column2: mainly to Indonesia from Amsterdam out. After that I was in aircraft sales (mainly Cessna's) and I flew a twin-engined Aerocommander for 7 years from Brussels as my home-base.

column1: Now I'm selling Frasca (American) Flight Simulators in all European countries except England
column2: Spain and Italy. In a few weeks time I shall go by car to France
column3: Switzerland and Austria
column4: — all in connection with my business. I intend to continue for at least one more year. I'm now 76
column5: and in good health fortunately. I'm feeling very sorry for Rolf
column6: who lost his wife not so long ago. His health does not seem to be very good at present.

column1: Please excuse me for typing this letter
column2: but I have a very bad handwriting.

column1: I shall be very pleased to hear from you and may be that we might even meet again
column2: be it in Europe or the USA. I doubt if I shall ever go to Chile. What was your latest contact with the Gräfin? I had still one or two letters from her after I had left Germany.

column1: My handwriting is really too bad!";Den Haag

column1: I'm working quite hard. The results are however rather meagre. This is mainly due to the recession in all the European countries. This coming week I may fly to Lyon to see a prospect. Flying in Europe is quite expensive as you may know.

column2: 26 January 1991.

column1: As I had nearly given up all hope of hearing from you
column2: I was extra pleased when your letter finally arrived. It arrived on the 10th of this month and I should have replied a bit earlier had it not been that my stomach ulcer was worrying me again. So every now and then
column3: usually not more then twice a year I feel my stomach. Merely by reducing my food to a minimum the pain usely disappears again after 2 – 4 weeks. This inconvenience is of course nothing in comparison with that of Rolf and your wife Angela.

column1: At the beginning of this month Rolf wrote me: ""Ich selbst bin wieder sehr krank am Herzen und leide an Blutarmut"". On my next trip to Switzerland I shall make a point of visiting him.

It was most interesting to read your curriculum and travel log. Most of my traveling is registered in my many logbooks. There is hardly a place in Europe to be found which I have not visited (with more than 100.000 inhabitants). I once counted the countries I have visited and I came to about 80. But I never visited South-America and I'm very tempted to do so. I may be visiting South-Africa in about November this year. I went to that country in 1938 and at the outbreak of World War II I did there my training for bomberpilot. Later on I was stationed there for KLM and was then flying Constellations from Johannesburg to Kano in Nigeria.
So should I go to South-Africa
column2: and I have at the moment the intention to do so
column3: I might be able to go on a charterflight from Johannesburg to Santiago. I wrote my friends in South-Africa to check if that would be possible.

column1: Last September I made a trip by car to Switzerland
column2: Austria and Hungary. On my way back
column3: I went through East-Germany and visited also Weimar. I had a look at the ""Haus in der Sonne"" and took a picture of it with a pocketcamera. It is now housing women
column4: belonging I believe
column5: to some Christian Society. I was at the time living on the top floor. Again I'm living on the topfloor but now in Den Haag (The Hague) or actually I'm living in Scheveningen
column6: a beachresort and a suburb of Den Haag. As I had not been using that particular camera for years
column7: I forgot to move my finger from the lens. Sorry. I know how good you were already at photographing in your German time and I noticed that you really achieved something in that respect.

column1: In November I went to Tenerife (Islas Canarias) for a short holiday. It is the best place in ""Europe"" for guaranteed sunshine.
I don't think that I told you that I never married. I now feel sorry that I didn't. I moved around too much.
Sorry to hear that Angela is still suffering. I do hope that she will soon recover.
Please write again. I shall also do it.
Kindest regards to you and Angela

column3: so he told me
column4: even if wishing to

I liked him so much with his clever dry humor and great common sense together with his friendly sensitive personality. I would have liked to know more about Rolf's covering the 52 years we lost contact. This I can't ask his sons Dieter and Sabin as I am not acquainted with them
column6: so I would be very grateful to you if you could provide me with such information.

column1: In my last letter to Rolf I described the interesting part of our Spring vacation. We drove 1000 km up Northern Chile and rented a Chevrolet jeep Trooper 4WD to Pan de Azucar a remarkable micro climate plateau
column2: located in the middle of a big desertic zone
column3: 800m high on the very abrupt edge that falls direct on the Pacific Ocean. At the surrounding desert it ocasionally
column4: every 8 to 10 years
column5: it gets flooded by terrible downpore of rain that fills the whole flattened 3/4 km wide river bed that runs flanked by steep inclines
column6: normaly all bone dry. It is over this sandy bed that you drive inland until we saw a rock formation that indicated we should turn 180 degrees back to the coast and clime a track up the incline until we got to the 800m high plateau over the ocean. Its surfase is undulating with grounded row white silica full of black cactus
column7: shrubs and low cushions of all kind of flowers (Spring). No human creature around for hundreds of km. Everything
column8: the spines of the cactus
column9: shrubs etc.. are dripping with moisture that equals a yearly rain fall of 1
column10: 20 meters. The micro climate is due to the moist satureted air that builds over tropical waters of the Pacific
column11: that convert to clouds when flowing over the cold Humbolt stream
column12: then when forced to clime the 800 high coast of Pan de Azucar the saturated clouds condence its water on every obstacle stands on their way and then falls as water drops to the ground. There is research going on where they ha-constructed a 10 m high by 4 to 5 m wide net that condences into water the moister of the cloudes when hitting the net. At the bottom
column13: the collectedted water is a constant one inch pipe that fill an overflowing tank of water where guanacos (wild llamas) come to drink. As nobody harms them
column14: you can get as close as 10 meters. While you are eating a sandwich foxes get close to your feet begging for crumbs of bread. Before they could drink out of that tank
column15: they used to lick the condenced water from the cactus spines and shrub branches. All this big desertic area is controled as a National Fauna and Flora Preservation Park and not allowed to go by yourself
column16: so if well recommended they provide you with a guide. The low passing clouds make visibility very difficult and this makes it very dangerous of getting lost
column17: and afraid of not seeing the edge of the cliffs and risk and falling direct the 800 m into the ocean.

column1: Sorry John all this too long description has become terribly boresome. It is the fault that I was taken away by this ""ganz unheimish"" adventure. You should come and stay with us! Best time is September to December. We have a small but very attractive apartment on a 18th floor with beutiful view over a Country Club
column2: where we play golf two or three times a week
column3: and over it you can see the big snow covered Andes. Our study has acomodation for two guests
column4: a bathroom and a closet.

column1: We took our Summer vacations rather late in March. As I had had been twice with pneumonia in a one year's period
column2: now I am very well luckely
column3: the physician recommended me to go for a few weeks to the country
column4: so as to be in good shape for Winter when Santiago gets terribly polluted with smog. So we went to the South Lake District. As it would have been a 10 hours drive
column5: and Angela can't drive any more because of her poor eyesight
column6: this would have been too much of a day's drive for me. Therefore we loaded the car and ourselves on train to Temuco. From there we drove to stay with several friends at their farms on Lake Villarrica
column7: and then to Osorno
column8: Lake Pullehue and another farm in Lake Rupanco
column9: that were 4 to 5 hours further South. From the living rooms over the lakes you could see the snow covered volcanos
column10: first Volcano Villarrica
column11: then at Rupanco Volcano Osorno and Cerro Punteagudo surrounded by virgin forests. On our return to Santiago we loaded our car again on the train in Osorno.

column1: I hope to hear from you soon
column2: and don't forget that you should come and visit us in Santiago. A lovely place for you to visit is the mountain range of Torres de Paine in Patagonia. Very much like the Dolomites
column3: but more impressive.

column3: Hemos realizado nuestros planes de los dos últimos años. column4: es Etienne el verdadero arquitecto de nuestra casa campestre. Tiene estilo típico de los Alpes Austriacos. Nos despedimos del Peru a fines de Abril ultimo. En Diciembre del año pasado nos despedimos de nuestra querida casa en Lima-Miraflores con el lindo matrimonio de nuestra hija con un joven alemán muy simpático que ella conoció en Lima dónde había trabajado ya tres años. De su trabajo lo mandaron a Hamburgo y nuestra hija se fué con él. La casa en Lima vendida y nuestros bultos despachados a Europa nos fuimos a Chaclacayo (a 30 km de Lima) a la casa de mi Mamá para hacer sus maletas

column12: con muchos amigos en el Aeropuerto no la conmovió!! Yo no podía ver por mis lagrimas! El viaje era muy lindo y el Bruno no sufrió exceptando que el ruido de los jets en Caracas y Lisboa donde yo pedi permiso para sacarlo..... le dieron nervios. Llegamos a Milano en los ultimos dias de Abril y descansamos como planeado cerca al lago Garda. El tiempo no era tan lindo como puede ser en esta época y después de 15 dias de descanso seguimos viaje con un carro alquilado en dirección a Austria. En Carinthia teniamos ya alquilado un departamentito cerca al lago donde alojarnos durante la ultima etapa de la construcción de nuestra casa. Todo hubiera sido muy bien
